# Authentication — Auth0

Konnect outsources every credential-related concern to **Auth0**. The platform never stores passwords, never issues JWTs, never seeds Identity roles, and never holds refresh-token state. The API only validates the bearer tokens Auth0 issues and reads the namespaced custom claims the Auth0 Actions stamp into them.

This page covers the tenant setup, the two Actions, the dev / prod configuration shape, and how the integration tests run without a live Auth0 tenant.

## Identifier model — single Guid generated by Auth0

Konnect's `User.Id` is a `Guid` — and that exact value is also stored in Auth0's `app_metadata.external_id`. Same value, same name, three locations:

```
Konnect entity:           User.Id (Guid)        ← internal source of truth
Auth0 app_metadata:       external_id: "<guid>" ← same value, same name
JWT custom claim:         https://konnect.dev/external_id ← stamped by Post-Login Action
```

The Pre-User-Registration Action generates the Guid before Auth0 finalises the new user. The Post-Login Action reads `app_metadata.external_id` and stamps it into every issued access token. Konnect's middleware reads the JWT claim, parses the Guid, and treats it as the primary key into the `users` table. No DB lookup just to translate `sub → User.Id` — the Guid is on the token.

## Audience-split — two APIs, two SPA clients

The seeker side and the employer side of the marketplace are isolated at the JWT-audience level. The same human can hold both identities simultaneously (they'd be two different active sessions, one per SPA), and a token minted for one side cannot be reused against the other.

| Auth0 object | Purpose |
|---|---|
| 1 Tenant | The whole IdP — `konnect-dev.au.auth0.com` for dev, separate prod tenant later |
| 2 APIs | `https://api.konnect.dev/seeker` and `https://api.konnect.dev/employer` — these become the JWT `aud` values |
| 2 SPA Applications | "Konnect Seeker Web" and "Konnect Employer Web" — no client_secret, browser-side PKCE |
| 2 Actions | Pre-User-Registration (Guid generation) + Post-Login (stamp role + Guid into JWT) |

## Setup — Auth0 CLI

One-time setup, run from a workstation logged into the target tenant via `auth0 login`.

```bash
# 1. Two API resources — these own the audience values that go into JWTs.
auth0 apis create --name "Konnect Seeker API" \
  --identifier "https://api.konnect.dev/seeker" --signing-alg RS256

auth0 apis create --name "Konnect Employer API" \
  --identifier "https://api.konnect.dev/employer" --signing-alg RS256

# 2. Two SPA Applications — no client_secret, browser-side PKCE.
auth0 apps create --name "Konnect Seeker Web" --type spa \
  --callbacks "http://localhost:3000/callback" \
  --logout-urls "http://localhost:3000" --web-origins "http://localhost:3000"

auth0 apps create --name "Konnect Employer Web" --type spa \
  --callbacks "http://localhost:3001/callback" \
  --logout-urls "http://localhost:3001" --web-origins "http://localhost:3001"
```

Capture the `client_id` of each SPA — you need them as Action secrets in step 4.

## Action 1 — Pre User Registration

Triggers: **Pre User Registration** flow. Fires for Database connection signups (email + password through Auth0 Universal Login). Does NOT fire for social-login signups — see "Scope" below.

```javascript
exports.onExecutePreUserRegistration = async (event, api) => {
  const externalId = crypto.randomUUID();
  api.user.setAppMetadata('external_id', externalId);
};
```

After Auth0 finalises the user record, `app_metadata.external_id` is permanently set to a fresh Guid. It will never change for the lifetime of that account.

## Action 2 — Post Login

Triggers: **Login / Post Login** flow. Runs on every login *and* every refresh-token exchange. Adds two custom claims to the access token under the `https://konnect.dev` namespace.

```javascript
exports.onExecutePostLogin = async (event, api) => {
  const namespace = 'https://konnect.dev';

  const externalId = event.user.app_metadata?.external_id;
  if (externalId) {
    api.accessToken.setCustomClaim(`${namespace}/external_id`, externalId);
  }

  if (event.client.client_id === event.secrets.SEEKER_CLIENT_ID) {
    api.accessToken.setCustomClaim(`${namespace}/role`, 'JobSeeker');
  } else if (event.client.client_id === event.secrets.EMPLOYER_CLIENT_ID) {
    api.accessToken.setCustomClaim(`${namespace}/role`, 'Recruiter');
  }
};
```

**Action Secrets** (configure in the Action editor, not in the source):

| Name | Value |
|---|---|
| `SEEKER_CLIENT_ID` | The `client_id` of the "Konnect Seeker Web" SPA |
| `EMPLOYER_CLIENT_ID` | The `client_id` of the "Konnect Employer Web" SPA |

Why namespaced URL claims (`https://konnect.dev/...`) — Auth0 requires every custom claim to be a URL-shaped name so it can never collide with reserved JWT claims (`role`, `email`, etc.). The URL doesn't have to resolve; it's just a stable identifier.

## What the JWT looks like

A seeker logging in via the Seeker SPA receives an access token with this payload:

```json
{
  "iss": "https://konnect-dev.au.auth0.com/",
  "sub": "auth0|65ab12cdef987654abcdef01",
  "aud": "https://api.konnect.dev/seeker",
  "azp": "kSeEkErClIeNtId123",
  "iat": 1714900000,
  "exp": 1714903600,
  "scope": "openid profile email",
  "https://konnect.dev/external_id": "8f3c4d7a-1b9e-4e2f-9c1a-7d6b5a4f3e2d",
  "https://konnect.dev/role": "JobSeeker"
}
```

The same human logging in via the Employer SPA gets a different token — same `sub`, different `aud`, different `https://konnect.dev/role`.

## Konnect-side pipeline

[Program.cs](https://github.com/win-son-dev/konnect-server/blob/main/Konnect.Platform/Konnect.WebAPI/Program.cs) wires the JwtBearer scheme against the Auth0 Authority and accepts both audiences. The bearer scheme handles signature / issuer / audience / expiry validation. After it runs, [`KonnectAuthenticationMiddleware`](https://github.com/win-son-dev/konnect-server/blob/main/Konnect.Platform/Konnect.WebAPI/Middleware/KonnectAuthenticationMiddleware.cs) confirms the two namespaced custom claims are present and well-formed; on failure it logs at `Warning` (not `Error` — these are operational events, not system faults) and returns 401.

```
Authorization: Bearer <jwt>
        ↓
JwtBearer middleware (framework)
  - fetches JWKS from https://konnect-dev.au.auth0.com/.well-known/jwks.json (cached)
  - validates iss / aud / exp / signature
  - populates HttpContext.User from payload claims
        ↓
KonnectAuthenticationMiddleware
  - confirms https://konnect.dev/external_id parses as Guid
  - confirms https://konnect.dev/role is JobSeeker or Recruiter
  - 401 + LogWarning on failure
        ↓
[Authorize(Roles = "JobSeeker")]
        ↓
Endpoint
```

## Configuration shape

Konnect's API never initiates logins and never calls the Auth0 Management API, so no `client_secret` lives anywhere in the WebAPI configuration — only the public OIDC discovery values.

```json
{
  "Auth0": {
    "Domain": "konnect-dev.au.auth0.com",
    "SeekerAudience": "https://api.konnect.dev/seeker",
    "EmployerAudience": "https://api.konnect.dev/employer"
  }
}
```

Dev values live in [`appsettings.Development.json`](https://github.com/win-son-dev/konnect-server/blob/main/Konnect.Platform/Konnect.WebAPI/appsettings.Development.json) and target a free Auth0 dev tenant. Prod values are injected from a secret store at deploy time.

The `Auth0:Domain` setting is validated at host startup in non-test environments — a missing or empty value fails the boot rather than letting the API run with broken auth.

## Scope: Database connection only

This foundation supports email + password authentication via Auth0 Universal Login (Database connection) only. **Social login (LinkedIn, Google, GitHub) is intentionally deferred** because Pre-User-Registration Actions only fire for Database connections — a social signup would arrive at Konnect with no `app_metadata.external_id`, no `https://konnect.dev/external_id` JWT claim, and the auth middleware would reject the request.

Adding social login later requires either:
- a Machine-to-Machine app + `IAuth0ManagementRepository` to write back `external_id` after a Konnect-side first-login provisioning, or
- a Custom Database connection with imported user_id control.

Tracked as a follow-up issue.

## Operational note — the Pre-User-Registration Action is a single point of failure

If the Pre-User-Registration Action breaks, regresses, or is disabled, new signups arrive in Auth0 successfully but with no `app_metadata.external_id`. Their JWTs lack the `https://konnect.dev/external_id` claim, the auth middleware rejects every request with 401, and they cannot use Konnect until the Action is fixed *and* the affected `app_metadata` rows are backfilled by hand.

Mitigations in place:
- Konnect logs the rejection at `Warning` with the literal claim name and a pointer at the Action so on-call can diagnose in seconds.
- Admin-created Auth0 users (created via the Auth0 dashboard or Management API directly, bypassing Universal Login) must have `app_metadata.external_id` seeded manually — Pre-User-Registration only fires for self-service signups.
- The integration test suite asserts that a token without `external_id` is rejected — regressing that path fails the build.

## Testing without a live Auth0 tenant

Integration tests under [`Konnect.Tests/WebAPI/Authentication/`](https://github.com/win-son-dev/konnect-server/tree/main/Konnect.Platform/Konnect.Tests/WebAPI/Authentication) exercise the full bearer pipeline without ever talking to Auth0:

| Component | What it does in tests |
|---|---|
| `TestJwtTokenFactory` | Owns an in-memory RSA keypair; mints test JWTs signed by it with whatever claims the test wants |
| `KonnectWebApplicationFactory` | Overrides `JwtBearerOptions.Configuration` to use the test signing key as the trusted JWKS — JwtBearer then accepts test-minted tokens as if they were from the real tenant |
| `AuthenticationPipelineTests` | Exercises the full pipeline against `/api/me` |
| `KonnectAuthenticationMiddlewareTests` | Pure unit tests of the claim-validation middleware via `DefaultHttpContext` |

CI runs these with no external state, no Testcontainers for the auth path, and no flaky network dependency.
